1: The queen of Sheba heard of Solomon's šēm (name/fame) connected with the name of YHWH and came to test him with ḥîḏôt (riddles, enigmatic questions — Judges 14:12-13; Proverbs 1:6 lists the ḥîḏāh alongside the māšāl as forms of wisdom discourse). The verb for 'test' (nāsāh) is the same used for Abraham's testing in Genesis 22:1 and Israel's testing in the wilderness (Exodus 17:2); here it is applied to Solomon's wisdom. The Queen of Sheba (ancient Sabaean kingdom, modern Yemen/southern Arabia) represents the farthest reaches of the known world coming to verify whether YHWH's wisdom is what is reported.
2: Her retinue is described as ḥayil (force/strength — used for an army), underscoring the scale. Her gifts — spices, gold, precious stones — mirror the tribute language of ancient Near Eastern diplomatic exchanges. Spices (bəśāmîm) from Arabia were one of the most valuable commodities of the ancient world; the phrase 'never again came such an abundance of spices' (v10) confirms the extraordinary nature of this gift.
3: Solomon answered all her dəḇārîm (words/questions) — the verse emphasizes comprehensiveness: 'there was nothing hidden from the king that he did not explain to her.' The rhetorical structure (she asked, he answered everything) frames Solomon's wisdom as having no limits or gaps — the apex of the gift given in 1 Kings 3.
4: She 'saw' (wattarʾ) all his wisdom and the house he built — the verb of witness is repeated emphatically in v5. The queen's encounter with Solomon's wisdom is an encounter with the visible embodiment of YHWH's gift; the architecture and administration are expressions of the wisdom, not merely its backdrop.
5: The list of what overwhelmed her — the food, the seating of his officials (mōšaḇ ʿăḇāḏāyw), the service of his attendants, their clothing (malbûšêhem), his cupbearers (mašqāyw), and the ascent by which he went up to YHWH's house — is a royal court portrait of astonishing order. The phrase 'there was no more spirit in her' (wəlōʾ-hāyāh bāh ʿôd rûaḥ) uses the same term rûaḥ for spirit/breath; she was literally breathless.
6: She begins her speech: 'The word (dāḇār) I heard in my own country about your deeds and your wisdom was true (ʾĕmet).' The word ʾĕmet (truth, faithfulness, reliability) is the same root as the covenant faithfulness word; she is acknowledging that the report was genuinely reliable.
7: 'The half was not told me' (lōʾ hûggad lî haḥăṣî) — a proverbial expression of how reality exceeds reputation. Her wisdom and prosperity (she brackets both) exceed their fame. The report structure (heard → came → saw) parallels the evangelistic pattern in Ruth 2:11 and will recur in NT missionary reports.
8: 'How fortunate (ʾašrê) are your men!' The Beatitude formula ʾašrê (blessed, happy, congratulated — the root of the Psalms' ʾašrê-sayings and Matthew 5:3-12) is applied to those who 'stand before you and hear your wisdom.' Proximity to wisdom produces well-being. The same logic appears in Proverbs 3:13 ('blessed is the man who finds wisdom') and Matthew 13:16-17.
9: The Queen of Sheba's doxology uses YHWH's covenant name: 'Praise YHWH your God (yəhî YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā bārûḵ).' A foreign queen pronounces a blessing on YHWH by name, acknowledging that Solomon's installation on the throne is an act of divine love: 'because YHWH has loved Israel forever' (bəʾahaḇat YHWH ʾet-yiśrāʾēl ləʿôlām). This anticipates the eschatological vision of the nations recognizing YHWH (Isaiah 2:2-4; Psalms 72:10-11).
10: She gave 120 talents of gold — an enormous amount. The 'abundance of spices' (bəśāmîm harbēh məʾōd) is emphasized as unprecedented: 'never again came such an abundance of spices as the Queen of Sheba gave to Solomon.' Archaeological records of the Sabaean kingdom confirm their role as major spice and incense traders along the Arabian trade routes.
11: Hiram's fleet returning from Ophir (an unidentified gold source, possibly in Arabia, East Africa, or India) brought ʿăṣê ʾalmûggîm (almugwood, possibly sandalwood or red sandalwood) — a rare exotic wood used for the temple and for musical instruments. The conjunction of temple construction materials with trade goods from multiple distant lands marks Solomon's project as an international undertaking.
12: The almugwood was used for misgərôt (supports, banisters) for the temple and palace, and for kinnōrôt wənəḇālîm (lyres and harps) for the musicians. These instruments become central to temple worship; the Levitical musical tradition established here (1 Chr 25) continues through the Psalms.
13: Solomon gave the queen 'all she desired and asked for, in addition to what he gave her from his royal bounty' (miyyaḏ hammelek, from the king's hand). The phrase 'all she desired' (kol-ḥepṣāh, all her desire/delight) is the same vocabulary used for the delight in wisdom (Proverbs 3:15, 1 Kings 8:11). The queen departs satisfied — carrying the gift of wisdom back to the ends of the earth.
14: The annual gold tribute: 666 talents. The specific number šēš mēʾôt šiššîm wāšēš kikkār (666 talents) invites comparison with Revelation 13:18; the more direct significance is that this verse begins the Deuteronomy 17:17 violation sequence. Deuteronomy 17:17 explicitly prohibits the king from accumulating silver and gold excessively; the narrative is documenting Solomon's systematic transgression of the Mosaic royal law.
15: The tribute calculation is explicit: in addition to regular merchants and trade revenue, revenues came from 'all the kings of Arabia' (malkê hāʿăraḇ) and from regional governors. The administrative apparatus for collecting this revenue mirrors the 12-district system of 1 Kings 4.
16: Two hundred large shields (ṣinnāh) of beaten gold — 600 shekels each — stored in the House of the Forest of Lebanon. The shields were display items demonstrating power; in 1 Kings 14:26 Shishak of Egypt will take them, and Rehoboam will replace them with bronze (1 Kings 14:27) — the progressive devaluation of Solomon's legacy is tracked through the fate of these shields.
17: Three hundred smaller shields (māgēn, a different shape from the ṣinnāh) at 3 minas each were stored in the same treasury. The transparency of this wealth accumulation is deliberate: the narrator documents the violation of Deuteronomy 17:17 in plain sight while the wealth is at its most impressive.
18: The great ivory throne overlaid with finest gold — the largest throne described in any ancient Near Eastern text. Ivory (šēn, tooth/tusk) from elephants was among the most precious materials in the ancient world; the throne's visual scale and material extravagance embody the Solomonic kingdom at its peak.
19: The throne's architectural details — six steps, rounded top, armrests, two lions flanking the seat — convey royal authority through symbolic form. Lions (ʾăryāyôt) symbolize royal power throughout the ancient Near East; twelve lions on the steps (v20) echo the twelve tribes over whom Solomon reigns.
20: 'Nothing like it had been made in any kingdom' (lōʾ-naʿăśāh kēn ləḵol-mamlāḵôt) — a superlative that marks the throne as unique among all human governments. The Solomonic narrative reaches its visual apex here; what follows in 1 Kings 11 is the moral unraveling of everything this throne represents.
21: All Solomon's drinking vessels were gold; silver was not considered valuable enough to use (ʾên kesep lōʾ ḥāšaḇ, silver was not counted). Silver as common as stones (v27) signals that extreme abundance has inverted normal economic hierarchies — a literary signal of approaching instability.
22: Tarshish ships (ʾŏnî tarshish — a fleet designed for long-distance ocean trade, named for Tarshish at the far western Mediterranean) returned every three years bringing 'gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks' (zāhāḇ wāḵesef šenhabbîm wəqôpîm wətukkiyyîm). The exotic cargo marks Solomon's commercial reach to the ends of the earth.
23: 'Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom' — a summary statement marking the absolute peak; after this, the downfall begins (1 Kings 11). The pairing of ʿōšer (riches) and ḥoḵmāh (wisdom) mirrors what YHWH gave in 1 Kings 3. The narrative has inverted the order: the riches now dominate the account.
24: 'All the earth sought the face of Solomon' (kol-hāʾāreṣ məḇaqqəšîm ʾet-pənê šəlōmōh) — the phrase 'seeking the face' (bāqaš pānîm) is normally used for seeking YHWH's presence (Psalms 27:8); here it is applied to Solomon. The nations are seeking Solomon's wisdom as if seeking the divine. The trajectory toward the Messianic vision (nations seeking YHWH) is present but distorted.
25: Annual gifts from all visitors included 'horses and mules' — central to the Deuteronomy 17:16 violation: 'He must not acquire many horses for himself, nor return the people to Egypt to acquire more horses.' Horses in vv26-29 are the next documented violation of the Mosaic royal law.
26: Solomon accumulated 1,400 chariots (reḵeḇ) and 12,000 horsemen (pārāšîm). Deuteronomy 17:16 prohibited the king from multiplying horses (lōʾ yarbeh-lô sûs); Solomon has done exactly this, creating the largest chariot force in Israel's history. The horse corps was stationed in chariot cities (ʿārê hāreḵeḇ) — a permanent military apparatus requiring ongoing taxation and labor to maintain.
27: 'The king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones' — the devaluation of precious metal marks the literary climax of Solomonic wealth, immediately before 1 Kings 11's moral reversal. This is the Deuteronomic wealth-prohibition fulfilled to its extreme opposite; the excess is documented as the setup for the fall.
28: Horses imported from Egypt (mimmitsrayim) and Kue (Cilicia, in southern Turkey) — the traders received them from Kue at a fixed price. This verse directly violates Deuteronomy 17:16's prohibition: 'nor shall he cause the people to return to Egypt in order to multiply horses.' The Mosaic law was designed to prevent Israel from reconstituting the Egyptian imperial military model; Solomon systematically recreates it.
29: A chariot from Egypt cost 600 shekels of silver; a horse 150. The royal traders exported them to 'all the kings of the Hittites and of the Arameans' — Solomon functions as an arms dealer in the ancient Near East's chariot trade. The same economic logic that concentrates wealth also concentrates the instruments of war, in direct contradiction to the king's covenant role as shepherd of a people who should trust YHWH rather than horses (Psalms 20:7; Isaiah 31:1).
